title: Bladimir | Teaching
text:
I am committed to transparency and accessibility in my work. In this section, you will find all the materials I have created for my teaching activities. If you have any questions about these materials, please feel free to contact me.

Teaching Philosophy

⬇ Download my Teaching Philosophy Statement
My teaching philosophy is grounded in the idea that learning is a cumulative and reflective process, where students build on prior knowledge to develop complex skills and concepts. As an instructor in quantitative methodology, I focus on bridging students’ existing knowledge with new material through individualized support, interactive review sessions, and open communication. My goal is to foster student autonomy by making statistical reasoning accessible, practical, and meaningful to each student’s own research interests and background.

  We teach what we do; we do what we teach.
  — George Cobb (2014)


Courses

I have not yet had the opportunity to design and implement a course from scratch. However, I have been actively involved in several undergraduate and graduate courses. In addition, while pursuing the Certificate in College Teaching at the University of Iowa, I created several documents that outline my principles and ideas about teaching.
Below is a list of the courses in which I have served as a teaching assistant. I am grateful to the professors who have placed their trust in my teaching skills and abilities, and who have also helped me grow and improve as an educator.

PSQF 6272: Clustered Multilevel Analysis
University of Iowa · Fall 2025 Supervisor: Dr. Lesa Hoffman
A course focused on multilevel analysis, its concepts, and applications in the social sciences. It gradually covers different types of designs, models, and fixed/random effects.

Graduate



PSQF 7375: Bayesian Psychometric Modeling
University of Iowa · Fall 2024 Supervisor: Dr. Jonathan Templin
A course focused on psychometric modeling using Bayesian methods. It covers the principles of the Bayesian framework and how to apply it to various statistical models.

Graduate



PSQF 6271: Longitudinal Multilevel Models
University of Iowa · Fall 2024 Supervisor: Dr. Lesa Hoffman
A course focused on multilevel analysis for longitudinal data, covering different types of variance-covariance structures and fixed/random effects.

Graduate



PSQF 6270: Generalized Linear Models
University of Iowa · Fall 2023, 2024, 2025 Supervisor: Dr. Lesa Hoffman
A course focused on generalized linear models, covering different types of models (e.g., logistic regression, Poisson regression) and their applications in the social sciences.

Graduate



PSQF 6249: Factor Analysis and Structural Equation Modeling
University of Iowa · Spring 2024 Supervisor: Dr. Lesa Hoffman
A course focused on factor analysis and structural equation modeling, covering different types of models (e.g., confirmatory factor analysis, path analysis) and their applications in the social sciences.

Graduate



PSQF 6246: Design of Experiments
University of Iowa · Fall 2022, 2023 Supervisor: Dr. Ariel Aloe
A course focused on the design of experiments, covering different types of experimental designs (e.g., randomized controlled trials, factorial designs) and their applications in the social and health sciences.

Graduate



Pedagogical Content Knowledge

In 2024, I worked on a short version of my Pedagogical Content Knowledge (PCK) for a quantitative methods course for social work students at my alma mater. This document outlines the key components of my PCK, including my understanding of the subject matter, my beliefs about teaching and learning, and my strategies for effectively conveying complex quantitative concepts to students with diverse backgrounds and levels of prior knowledge.
⬇ Download the PCK document
↑ Back to top

title: Bladimir | Publications
text:
I began working in research in 2015, during my third year of the Social Work program at the Universidad del Bío-Bío. My first position was as a research assistant at the Centro de Investigación Educativa CIDCIE, after being invited by Professor Carlos Rodríguez Garcés — my supervisor, mentor, and dear friend.
Since then, I have worked on many projects, particularly those related to education, although I have also enjoyed studying topics related to public safety and the philosophy of Michel Foucault — a more personal intellectual passion that I have not yet published on.
In this section, you will find a selection of my publications, including journal articles, book chapters, and my master’s thesis. For a complete list and full-text access, visit my ResearchGate profile.

Selected Publications



Padilla, B., Dorman, E., & Hoffman, L. (2026). Multilevel analysis for quantitative communication scientists. In Handbook of quantitative research methods in communication science.


Padilla, B., Hoffman, L., & Suazo, C. (2025). Socioeconomic factors and school context in math admission test scores in Chile: A multilevel analysis. Perspectiva Educacional, 64(3). https://doi.org/10.4151/07189729-Vol.64-Iss.3-Art.1617


Padilla, G., Rodríguez, C., & Espinosa, D. (2023). Evaluación docente y portafalio: Análisis del desempeño profesional docente en Chile. Revista Educación, 32(63). https://doi.org/10.18800/educacion.202302.A004


Padilla, G., Suazo, C., & Rodríguez, C. (2023). Seguimiento al Sistema de Admisión Escolar en Chile: ¿Cómo ha funcionado el aseguramiento de la inclusión? Revista de La Escuela de Ciencias de La Educación, 1(18), 17–38.



Thesis


Master’s Thesis
Padilla, B. (2025). Conflated Random Slopes in Multilevel Analysis. University of Iowa.

title: Bladimir | About
text:
Bladimir Padilla
    PhD Candidate · Psychological & Quantitative Foundations · University of Iowa

    
      Hi! I am a graduate student in Educational Measurement and Statistics, broadly interested in
      multilevel and longitudinal analyses, psychometrics, and causal inference. My work sits at
      the intersection of educational outcomes and quantitative methods, with a particular focus
      on Chilean higher education.
    

    
      Before graduate school, I studied Social Work at the Universidad del Bío-Bío, Chile, and
      worked as a researcher at the Centro de Investigación Educativa CIDCIE at the same university.
    

    Research Interests
    Tools: Multilevel Analysis · Longitudinal Analysis · Causal Inference · Psychometrics
    Topics: Higher Education Access & Equity · Educational Quality & School Climate · Quantitative Methods

    News & Updates
    News coming soon.

title: Bladimir | Research
text:
My research involves two main areas: tools (i.e., quantitative methods) and topics (i.e., substantive areas). I enjoy studying the tools I use in research, particularly their history, characteristics, advantages, and limitations. I am passionate about making these methods accessible through practical examples, and I enjoy working on tutorials and how-to projects. As for the topics, I have been primarily involved in higher education, studying performance on standardized tests and admission to selective higher education institutions.
In this section, you will find some of the projects I am currently working on.

Working Papers


Multilevel Analysis of PAES 2025 Results
Paper dedicated to the multilevel analysis of PAES 2025 test scores in reading proficiency and mathematics proficiency (M1).
Draft available upon request


Exploring the Probabilities of Being Admitted to Selective Universities in Chile in 2022
Rodríguez, C., Padilla, B., Mojoyinola, M., & Romero, D.
This paper analyzes the admission results of Chilean students who participated in the selective higher education admission process in 2022 by means of a binary logistic regression model using academic and non-academic predictors. The results show that non-academic attributes were relevant predictors of the probability of admission into selective universities, especially the students’ family income quintile and school sector, which challenges the principle of fairness among students.

Under Review



Works in Progress


On the Implications of Adjusting for Confounders, Colliders, and Mediators: A Tutorial Using Propensity Scores and ANCOVA
Padilla, B., & Aloe, A.
This paper presents arguments and examples regarding the precautions that researchers interested in using propensity scores and ANCOVA should consider in accordance with the presence of confounders, colliders, and mediators in their causal models.

Early Stage



Multilevel Analysis for Longitudinal Data
Padilla, B., & Hoffman, L.
Book chapter dedicated to multilevel modeling approaches for intensive longitudinal data, focusing on core concepts and offering recommendations on current best practices.

Early Stage
